title: Quote Templates, Dynamic Content & PDF Output
description: Build professional quote documents with dynamic content, conditional sections, branding, and multi-format output.

sections.title: Document Generation Overview
sections.content:
The final step of the quoting process is producing a professional document that can be sent to the customer. SAP CPQ allows you to define dynamically populated documents that pull data directly from the quote — products, prices, customer information, terms, and custom fields — into a pre-designed template.

Documents are generated by executing the Generate Documents action on a quote. This action is a non-transitional workflow action (it produces output without changing the quote status by default). The output is always generated for the current active revision of the quote.

SAP CPQ supports three output formats: PDF (the most common for customer-facing proposals), Microsoft Word (useful when the customer or internal team needs to edit the document), and Microsoft Excel (useful for detailed pricing breakdowns or data-heavy appendices — Excel output supports formulas but not macros, as only .xls and .xlsx formats are allowed).

After generation, the user has three options: download the document to their local machine, return to the quote to continue working, or send the document by email directly from CPQ to a colleague or customer. Generated documents are also stored on the quote's Documents tab for later retrieval.

By default, the quote remains fully editable after document generation. However, best practice is to configure the workflow so that generating a document triggers a status change (e.g. from "Preparing" to "Sent"), which then disables editing. If further changes are needed, the user creates a new revision.

sections.title: Template Structure & the Template Editor
sections.content:
Quote document templates are created and managed under Setup → Quote → Document Generation Templates. The template editor provides a rich-text environment where administrators build the document layout, mixing static content with dynamic tags.

A template is composed of sections — logical blocks of content such as a cover page, introduction, product table, pricing summary, terms and conditions, and signature block. Each section can be individually controlled for visibility (shown or hidden based on conditions), making the same template adaptable to different quote scenarios.

The template editor supports standard formatting: fonts, sizes, colours, bold/italic/underline, alignment, tables, images, and page breaks. Templates can include a company logo, consistent colour schemes, and standardised page layouts — all essential for producing professional, brand-compliant proposals.

Multiple templates can exist in a single CPQ tenant. Different templates can be assigned to different markets, product types, or user types — so a quote for the European market uses a different template (language, legal terms, currency format) than one for North America. The user or the system selects the appropriate template at generation time.

Templates support translations — you can create language-specific versions of the same template and assign them based on the user's or customer's locale. This is essential for global deployments where quotes must be presented in the customer's language.

sections.title: Dynamic Content — Tags, Loops & Conditional Sections
sections.content:
The power of CPQ document generation lies in dynamic content — tags that are replaced with actual quote data at generation time. Tags use the same Formula Builder syntax found throughout CPQ, wrapped in <* ... *> delimiters.

Header-level tags pull data from the quote header: quote number, creation date, effective date, expiration date, customer name, customer address, market, currency, totals, and custom field values. Example: <*CTX( Quote.QuoteName )*> inserts the quote name, <*CTX( Quote.Customer(BillTo).Company )*> inserts the bill-to customer's company name.

Line-item loops iterate over quote items to generate a product table. A loop tag defines the start and end of the repeating section, and item-level tags within the loop pull each product's name, description, quantity, unit price, discount, and extended amount. This means the product table automatically grows or shrinks to match the number of items on the quote.

Conditional sections use IF/ENDIF logic to show or hide parts of the template based on conditions. For example: show a subscription terms section only if the quote contains recurring items; show a discount justification section only if the total discount exceeds 10%; or hide the cost breakdown for customers who should only see the final price. Conditions can reference any data accessible through CTX tags.

Quote table tags can pull data from quote tables into the document. This allows information like shipping addresses, tender offerings, or service level details stored in quote tables to be included in the generated output alongside standard quote data.

sections.title: Branding, Formatting & Professional Output
sections.content:
A quote document is often the first tangible deliverable a customer receives from your company. Its quality directly influences the customer's perception of professionalism and attention to detail.

Logo and header/footer. Templates should include the company logo in a consistent position (typically top-left or centred at the top). Headers and footers can contain the company name, document title, page numbers, confidentiality notices, and the quote reference number. These elements repeat on every page and create visual consistency.

Colour scheme. Use the company's brand colours for headings, table headers, accent lines, and highlighted values. Avoid overusing colour — a two-colour scheme (primary brand colour + neutral/grey) is typically sufficient for a professional look.

Typography. Use no more than two fonts: one for headings (bolder, slightly larger) and one for body text (clean, readable). Standard business fonts work best — they render consistently across different systems and PDF viewers.

Table formatting. Product tables should have clear column headers, alternating row colours (optional, for readability), right-aligned numbers, and consistent decimal formatting. Include subtotals, discount rows, and a prominent grand total. Currency symbols should match the quote's market currency.

Page layout. Define consistent margins, use page breaks between major sections (e.g. cover page, product details, terms), and ensure the document flows logically from introduction through products and pricing to terms and signature. Templates with well-structured page breaks produce clean output even for quotes with many line items.

sections.title: Multi-Language Quotes & Global Deployments
sections.content:
Global organisations need to generate quotes in the customer's language, not just the sales rep's language. SAP CPQ supports this through a combination of template translations, product translations, attribute translations, and dictionary-based UI translations.

Template translations. You can create multiple language versions of the same template. Each version contains the same structure and tags but with translated static text (section headings, legal terms, introductory paragraphs, labels). The correct version is selected based on the user's language setting, the customer's locale, or a custom field on the quote.

Product and attribute translations. Product names, descriptions, attribute names, and attribute value labels can all be translated using CPQ's built-in translation mechanism (Setup → Import/Export for bulk operations, or the "Translate this item" option on individual records). When a document is generated, the translated values are pulled based on the active language context.

Dictionary-based translations. Custom field labels, column headers, and UI elements are translated via custom dictionaries assigned to users. A French dictionary assigned to a French user ensures all dynamic labels in the generated document appear in French.

For the exam, remember that multi-language support requires effort across three layers: the template itself (static text), the product data (names and descriptions), and the dictionaries (field labels and UI elements). Missing any layer results in a partially translated document — a common implementation mistake.

sections.title: Document Generation Best Practices & Exam Tips
sections.content:
Document generation is often underestimated in CPQ projects. Here are the patterns that lead to successful implementations:

Start with the customer's expectation. Before building templates, collect examples of proposals the client currently sends to customers. Match the existing format as closely as possible — this reduces change-management friction and ensures the CPQ output feels familiar to both sales reps and customers.

Keep templates modular. Use conditional sections to build one flexible template rather than maintaining many separate templates for different scenarios. A single template with show/hide logic for subscription vs one-time products, different legal terms per region, and optional sections for different product types is easier to maintain than five separate templates.

Test with realistic data. Templates that look perfect with 3 line items often break with 30. Test with quotes that have many items, long product names, high quantities, large discount values, and multiple pages. Check page breaks, table overflow, and column alignment at scale.

Version your templates. CPQ does not have built-in template versioning. Maintain backups of working templates externally (e.g. in SharePoint or a version control system) before making changes. A broken template in production means no quotes can be generated until it is fixed.

Workflow integration. Configure the workflow so that document generation triggers a status change (e.g. to "Sent") and optionally sends a notification. This creates a clear audit trail: the document was generated, the status changed, and the customer was notified — all tracked by the system.

Key exam points: output formats are PDF, Word, and Excel. Documents are generated for the active revision. The Generate Documents action is non-transitional by default. Tags use the Formula Builder / CTX syntax. Templates support conditional sections with IF/ENDIF. Multi-language requires template translations + product translations + dictionaries.

questions.text: Which output formats does SAP CPQ support for document generation?
options: PDF only
PDF and Word only
PDF, Word, and Excel
PDF, Word, Excel, and PowerPoint
questions.explanation: SAP CPQ supports three output formats: PDF (most common for proposals), Word (when editing is needed), and Excel (for detailed data — supports formulas but not macros). PowerPoint is not supported.

questions.text: The Generate Documents action is a transitional action that automatically changes the quote's status.
questions.explanation: Generate Documents is a non-transitional action by default — it produces the output without changing the quote status. However, best practice is to configure the workflow so that document generation is followed by a status transition (e.g. to "Sent").

questions.text: Where are document generation templates created and managed in SAP CPQ?
options: Setup → Workflow/Approval → Templates
Setup → Quote → Document Generation Templates
Setup → Develop → Output Templates
Setup → General → Document Settings
questions.explanation: Templates are managed under Setup → Quote → Document Generation Templates. The template editor provides a rich-text environment for building layouts with static content and dynamic tags.

questions.text: Dynamic tags in document templates use the same Formula Builder / CTX syntax found throughout SAP CPQ.
questions.explanation: Document template tags use the standard <* ... *> syntax with Formula Builder variables and CTX tags. This consistency means the same tag knowledge used in rules, pricing, and custom fields applies to document generation.

questions.text: How does a document template generate a product table that grows or shrinks based on the number of quote items?
options: The admin manually adds rows for each expected product
A line-item loop tag iterates over quote items, repeating the row template for each item
A Python script generates the table externally and inserts it into the template
The template automatically detects items and creates rows without any special configuration
questions.explanation: A line-item loop tag defines a repeating section that iterates over all quote items. Item-level tags within the loop pull each product's data (name, quantity, price, etc.). The table automatically matches the number of items on the quote.

questions.text: Conditional sections in document templates can show or hide content based on IF/ENDIF logic referencing quote data.
questions.explanation: Templates support IF/ENDIF conditional sections that show or hide content based on conditions. Conditions can reference any quote data via CTX tags — for example, showing subscription terms only when recurring items exist, or hiding cost breakdowns for certain customers.

questions.text: After generating a document, which options are available to the user?
options: Download only — the quote is locked immediately
Download, return to the quote, or send the document by email
The document is automatically emailed to the customer with no user intervention
Download or delete — no email option is available
questions.explanation: After generation, the user has three options: download to their local machine, return to the quote to continue working, or send by email directly from CPQ. Generated documents are also stored on the quote's Documents tab.

questions.text: Excel output from SAP CPQ document generation supports VBA macros.
questions.explanation: Excel output supports formulas but not macros, as only .xls and .xlsx formats are allowed. A workaround using Excel add-ins exists for scenarios that require macro-like functionality.

questions.text: What are the three layers that must be translated for a fully multi-language quote document?
options: Template static text, approval rules, and workflow actions
Template static text, product/attribute data, and dictionaries (field labels)
Template static text, pricebook entries, and market definitions
Template static text, email notifications, and user records
questions.explanation: Multi-language documents require translations across three layers: the template (static text like headings and legal terms), the product data (product names, descriptions, attribute values), and the dictionaries (custom field labels and UI elements). Missing any layer results in a partially translated document.

questions.text: SAP CPQ has built-in template versioning that automatically tracks changes to document generation templates.
questions.explanation: CPQ does not have built-in template versioning. Templates should be backed up externally (e.g. in SharePoint or version control) before making changes. A broken template in production prevents all document generation until fixed.

questions.text: Which of the following is a recommended best practice for document template design?
options: Create a separate template for every possible quote scenario
Use one flexible template with conditional sections to handle different scenarios
Avoid using dynamic tags and manually enter data into each generated document
Generate documents only in PDF format to prevent customer edits
questions.explanation: Best practice is to build one flexible template with conditional show/hide sections rather than maintaining many separate templates. A single modular template with IF/ENDIF logic for different product types, regions, and terms is far easier to maintain.

questions.text: Data stored in quote tables can be included in generated quote documents using dedicated tags.
questions.explanation: Quote table tags allow data from quote tables (shipping addresses, tender details, service levels) to be pulled into the generated document alongside standard quote data. This extends the document's content beyond header and line-item data.

questions.text: A quote has three revisions: v1 (inactive), v2 (inactive), and v3 (active). Which revision is used when the user clicks Generate Documents?
options: v1 — the original version is always used
v2 — the most recently modified version is used
v3 — documents are always generated for the current active revision
All three — a separate document is generated for each revision
questions.explanation: Documents are always generated for the current active revision. Only one revision can be active at a time. To generate a document for an older revision, it must first be reactivated.

questions.text: Different document templates can be assigned to different markets, product types, or user types within the same CPQ tenant.
questions.explanation: Multiple templates can exist in a single tenant, and different templates can be assigned based on market, product type, or user type. This allows region-specific formatting, language, legal terms, and branding to be applied automatically.
